title: Gastroenterology
flags: Haemochromatosis: 'Common in caucasian populatio' appears truncated in the source.
Ascites: 'SAAG calculated by Albumin in ascitic fluid - Albumin in serum' should be verified before studying (rewording risks changing meaning).
PBC: '10% have RUQ' appears incomplete in the source.
Liver cirrhosis: 'Transient Elastography (Fibroscan) s the test...' contains a typo/unclear wording.
C. diff: 'First line is oral vancomycin for 1- days' has an incomplete duration.
Acute pancreatitis: 'Amylase is one useful for diagnosis' is unclear source wording.
UC section: the 'First line according to predominant symptom' block reads like IBS management and was kept as written — verify source context before using it for UC revision.
UGIB table: 'Fresh blood, melon' was preserved exactly but appears unclear (likely 'melena').
UGIB: 'Rockall score after endoscopy: % of rebreeding and mortality' contains typo/unclear wording.
Variceal hemorrhage: 'decrease vatical pressure' appears typo-heavy.
HCC: 'surgical reaction' appears unclear in the source (likely 'resection').
Refeeding syndrome: 'Torsades des pointes secondary to hypomagnesemia is a consequence of referring' appears typo-heavy.
NASH line appears incomplete: 'NASH is a type of NAFLD (non-alcoholic fatty liver'.
Coeliac: 'IgA (which can give a false positive TTG result)' was preserved exactly but should be verified before studying.
Dubin-Johnson: 'Causes a defection...' has typo-heavy wording (likely 'defect').

notes.body:
- AR genetic disorder causing too much iron absorption (stored in organs)
- Mutations in **HFE** gene on both copies of chromosome 6
- Common in caucasian populatio
- Sx: fatigue, joint pain, bronze skin darkening, DM
- Cardiac failure (secondary to DCM)
- Hypogonadism (secondary to cirrhosis and pituitary dysfunction; hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism)
- Arthritis
- Screening: transferrin saturation and ferritin (transferrin sat will be higher than ferritin)
- If in the family: **HFE** genetic testing

Complications:
- Reversible → cardiomyopathy and skin pigmentation
- Irreversible → liver cirrhosis, DM, hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism, arthropathy

Management (C282Y homozygous):
- First line: venesection (weekly, then monthly once levels below 50)
- Second line: Desferrioxamine

- **AR** disorder — ATP7B gene mutation
- Impaired copper excretion
- Young patient with hepatic and neurological features
- **Low caeruloplasmin and total serum copper**
- Kayser-Fleischer rings

- Chronic liver disorder in middle-aged females (9:1 F:M)
- Autoimmune relation: **Sjogren's in 80%**, RA, systemic sclerosis, thyroid disease
- **Interlobular bile ducts** damaged by chronic inflammation → progressive cholestasis → may progress to cirrhosis
- Decreased bile flow (cholestasis) → impaired fat absorption → fat-soluble vitamin deficiency
- Dense lymphoid infiltrates of hepatic portal tracts with hepatocyte necrosis
- 20-fold increased risk of HCC

Features:
- Early asymptomatic with raised ALP
- Classic feature (middle-aged woman): **pruritus**
- Cholestatic jaundice; hyperpigmentation over pressure points
- **Xanthelasma**, xanthomata, clubbing, hepatosplenomegaly
- 10% have RUQ

Diagnosis:
- Anti-mitochondrial Ab M2 subtype in 98%; ANA can be positive; smooth muscle Ab in 30%
- Raised serum **IgM**
- Abdominal US before diagnosis to exclude extrahepatic biliary obstruction

Management:
- 1st line: Ursodeoxycholic acid (slows progression, improves symptoms)
- Pruritus: Cholestyramine; fat-soluble vitamin supplementation
- Liver transplant if bilirubin >100 (graft recurrence can occur but not a problem)

Complications: cirrhosis → portal HTN → ascites/variceal haemorrhage; osteomalacia and osteoporosis; 20-fold HCC risk

- Biliary disease of unknown aetiology: inflammation and fibrosis of **intra and extra hepatic bile ducts**
- 4% of UC patients have PSC; 80% of PSC patients have **UC**. **Also associated with HIV**

Features: cholestasis (jaundice, pruritus, raised bilirubin & ALP); RUQ pain and fatigue (can be isolated fatigue)

Diagnosis/imaging:
- MRCP is diagnostic choice → multiple biliary strictures (beaded appearance)
- ERCP was previous gold standard but higher risk of pancreatitis
- p-ANCA can be positive
- Liver biopsy limited role, may show fibrosis / obliterative cholangitis ('onion skin')

Complications: cholangiocarcinoma in 10%; increased colorectal cancer risk

- Excess collagen and extracellular matrix deposition in periportal and pericentral zones → regenerative nodules
- Cirrhotic ascites: **should be given an aldosterone antagonist (Spironolactone)**

Scoring systems:
- Child-Pugh (severity)
- MELD: Model for ESLD (transplant waiting list)
- Transient Elastography (Fibroscan) to diagnose and monitor severity

- Poor prognosis sign: ascites

- Abnormal fluid accumulation in the abdomen
- SAAG (Serum-Ascites Albumin Gradient) = albumin in serum − albumin in ascitic fluid

| SAAG >11 g/L — Portal HTN (transudate) | SAAG <11 g/L (exudate) |
| --- | --- |
| Liver disease (MC): **alcohol, acute liver failure, liver mets** | Nephrotic syndrome (urinary protein loss) |
| Right heart failure / constrictive pericarditis | Hypoalbuminaemia / severe malnutrition |
| Budd-Chiari (hepatic vein thrombosis) — Doppler US | Hepatocellular carcinoma |
| Portal vein thrombosis, veno-occlusive disease, myxoedema | Malignancy (peritoneal), TB peritonitis, acute pancreatitis, bowel obstruction |

Management / next steps:
- Reduced dietary sodium; fluid restriction if Na <125 mmol/L
- Aldosterone antagonists (Spironolactone); add loop diuretic if no response
- Drainage if tense ascites (therapeutic paracentesis)
- If large volume (>5L): albumin cover to reduce paracentesis-induced circulatory dysfunction/mortality
- Prophylactic ABx to reduce SBP risk (**Cipro** if cirrhosis and ascites with protein of 15 g/L)
- TIPS may be considered

- Kidney impairment: ascites, low urine output, increased creatinine
- Managed with Terlipressin (vasopressin analogue)
- Induces **splanchnic vasoconstriction** to reduce portal pressure and improve renal blood flow
- Type 1: rapid (<2 weeks), usually following UGIB
- Type 2: gradual decline in renal function

- **MC cause of HCC in the UK is Hep C**

Assessed by Child-Pugh scoring:
- A (no portal HTN, single lesion <2cm): surgical resection
- A & B (2-3 tumours <3cm or 1 tumour <5cm WITHOUT invasion): transplant
- A or B (good status but spread): Sorafenib
- C (ESLD): symptomatic/supportive care

Liver failure mechanisms:
- After cardiac arrest → ischaemic hepatitis
- Paracetamol overdose → necrosis affecting the entire panacinar
- Viral hepatitis → apoptosis (possibility of regeneration)

- Cholangitis: US is the first line
- Liver abscess: empirical antibiotics — amoxicillin, ciprofloxacin, metronidazole
- SBP treatment: IV Cefotaxime (or any 3rd-gen cephalosporin); MC organism E. coli
- NAFLD next step: enhanced liver fibrosis; management weight loss → Semaglutide
- NASH (non-alcoholic steatohepatitis) is a type of NAFLD; associated with metabolic syndrome
- Alcoholic hepatitis: corticosteroids
- Hepatic encephalopathy: Lactulose and Rifaximin (also common after TIPSS)
- **Autoimmune hepatitis: IgG elevated / smooth muscle antibodies**
- Gilbert's syndrome: UGT1A1
- Dubin-Johnson syndrome (AR): defect in canalicular multispecific organic anion transporter (MRP2) → impaired excretion of conjugated bilirubin → conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia; benign

| Factor | Behaviour in liver disease |
| --- | --- |
| Factor VIII | Increased (reduced clearance by dysfunctional liver) |
| Factor II (PT), VII, IX | Vitamin-K dependent, made by liver → decreased in impaired synthesis |
| Factor XII | Synthesised by liver → decreased in impaired function |

| Drug category | Drugs |
| --- | --- |
| DILI | Paracetamol; Sodium Valproate; Phenytoin; MAOIs; Halothane; anti-TB (isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide); Statins; Alcohol; Amiodarone; Methyldopa; Nitrofurantoin |
| Cholestasis | **cOCP**; Flucloxacillin, Co-amoxiclav, Erythromycin; anabolic steroids/testosterones; phenothiazines (Chlorpromazine, Prochlorperazine); sulphonylureas (Gliclazide); Fibrates |
| Cirrhosis | Methotrexate; Methyldopa; Amiodarone |

Liver biopsy contraindications: deranged clotting (INR >1.4); low platelets (<60); anaemia; extrahepatic biliary obstruction; hydatid cyst; haemangioma; uncooperative patient; ascites

- Achalasia: dysphagia to BOTH solids and liquids
- Ix: oesophageal manometry (excessive tone), barium swallow (bird's beak), fluid level on CXR
- Treatment: balloon dilation

- Excess gastrin from a gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumour
- Majority in first part of duodenum (G cells), then pancreas; rarely ileum/jejunum/pylorus/3rd part of duodenum (no specialised G cells there)
- 30% of gastrinomas are part of MEN I
- Features: diarrhoea, malabsorption, multiple gastroduodenal ulcers
- Dx: **fasting gastrin levels with secretin stimulation test**
- MC site for gastrinomas: first part of duodenum

- Haematemesis (coffee ground), melena
- High urea correlates with UGIB (e.g. variceal bleeds)

| Oesophageal cause | Feature |
| --- | --- |
| Varices | Fresh blood, melon |
| Oesophagitis | Fresh blood streaks |
| Cancer | Dysphagia, weight loss |
| Mallory-Weiss | Repeated vomiting |

| Gastric cause | Feature |
| --- | --- |
| Ulcer | Low-volume bleed + IDA |
| Cancer | Frank haematemesis, dyspepsia |
| Dieulafoy | AV malformation, large-volume bleed |
| Erosive gastritis | Haematemesis + epigastric discomfort, NSAID use |

- Duodenal ulcer → haematemesis, melena, epigastric discomfort
- AV fistula → previous AAA surgery is a RF

Management / resuscitation:
- Glasgow-Blatchford score for OPAT vs inpatient; Rockall score after endoscopy (% rebleeding and mortality)
- Endoscopy immediately if severe; all within 24h
- Platelets if <50 and bleeding; FFP if fibrinogen <1 g/L or PT/APTT >1.5x; prothrombin complex concentrate if on warfarin and bleeding
- Non-variceal bleeding: PPIs
- Persistent bleeding despite repeated endoscopy → surgery

- Most significant complication of portal HTN from cirrhosis; risk of rupture → UGIB

Acute treatment:
- ABC resuscitation +/- blood transfusion; correct clotting (FFP, Vit K, platelets)
- **Terlipressin** (acts on V1-R → splanchnic vasoconstriction → decreases portal blood flow/pressure). S/E: hypertension, hyponatraemia, mesenteric ischaemia
- Prophylactic ABx to **reduce mortality** (usually quinolones)
- Endoscopic variceal **band ligation** (better than sclerotherapy)
- Sengstaken-Blakemore tube if uncontrolled
- **TIPSS** if everything else fails (connects hepatic vein to portal vein; common complication hepatic encephalopathy)

Prophylaxis:
- Non-selective beta-blockers (Carvedilol lowers hepatic venous pressure gradient; propranolol in decompensated cirrhosis)
- Endoscopic variceal band ligation (2-week intervals until eradication)

- Long-standing GORD = Barrett's = cancer (adenocarcinoma, signet ring cells)
- Gold standard reflux test: 24h oesophageal pH monitoring
- Barrett's oesophagus: metaplasia of lower oesophageal mucosa to columnar epithelium; managed with high-dose PPIs + endoscopic surveillance
- For oesophageal and gastric cancer: **endoscopic ultrasound is used to assess mural invasion**
- Oesophageal cancer is NOT associated with H. pylori (most commonly associated with ulceration)
- Boerhaave's syndrome: spontaneous oesophageal rupture; subcutaneous emphysema (palpable crepitus); CT with contrast is a must
- Pernicious anaemia (autoimmune): caused by intrinsic factor deficiency
- Plummer-Vinson syndrome (Paterson-Brown-Kelly): IDA + dysphagia (oesophageal webs) + atrophic glossitis (low Hb and MCV)

Eosinophilic oesophagitis management:
- PPI; swallowed topical corticosteroids; six-food elimination diet
- **Fluticasone**

| H. pylori topic | Note |
| --- | --- |
| MALT lymphoma treatment | H. pylori eradication |
| Interfering meds | ABx stopped 4 weeks before |
| Interfering meds | PPIs / Ranitidine stopped within 2 weeks |
| Interfering meds | Gaviscon / cOCP have no relation to 13C-UBT |

- Vascular deformity of GIT predisposing to IDA; mostly elderly
- Associated with aortic stenosis (ejection systolic murmur, best heard on expiration)
- Repeated scopes with no findings
- Features: anaemia, GI bleeding (UGIB melena, LGIB fresh red PR bleeding)
- Dx: colonoscopy; mesenteric angiography if acute bleeding; capsule OGD if repeated

Management:
- Active bleeding → APC (argon plasma coagulation) [cautery]
- Unstable/failed APC → mesenteric angiography with embolisation
- Refractory/massive → surgery (bowel resection)
- Recurrent/less severe → antifibrinolytics (TXA) or hormonal
- Supportive iron and blood transfusion

Pathophysiology:
- Hereditary: inheritance of APC gene
- Sporadic: allelic loss of APC; deletion of p53; deletion of DCC; activation of K-ras
- Only 5-15% of positive faecal blood have colorectal cancer
- Associated with endometrial cancer
- MC inherited colorectal Ca: HNPCC

- Peutz-Jeghers: **AD disease with development of non-cancerous hamartomatous polyps in the GI tract**; hyperpigmented macules on lips/oral mucosa; IDA; intestinal obstruction; 50% die from cancer (MC GI) by 60
- **Anal cancer is strongly associated with HPV16**
- Villous adenoma: triad of chronic diarrhoea, hypokalaemia, dehydration

IBS:
- Limit wholemeal bread and cereals high in bran
- New guidelines suggest hypnotherapy
- Avoid lactulose (bloating and flatulence)
- Acute anal fissure (<1 week): bulk-forming laxatives and diet, then topical glyceryl trinitrate if no use

| Laxative class | Examples |
| --- | --- |
| Osmotic | Lactulose; Macrogols; rectal phosphates |
| Stimulant | Senna; Docusate; Bisacodyl; Glycerol; Co-Danthramer |
| Bulk forming | Ispaghula husk; Methylcellulose |
| Faecal softener | Arachis oil |

- **Co-Danthramer is only prescribed to palliative patients** due to its **carcinogenic potential** (severe opioid-induced constipation not responding). Dantron stimulates colonic motility; Poloxamer softens. Pigment-laden macrophages = laxative abuse

- Only involves the mucosal layer; MC site rectum; goblet cell depletion; crypt abscesses
- A flare-up can be triggered by smoking cessation

| Truelove-Witts (stools/day) | Severity / management |
| --- | --- |
| <4 | mild: oral aminosalicylate |
| 4-6 | moderate: topical rectal aminosalicylates |
| >6 (+pyrexia, tachycardia, anaemia, raised markers) | severe: IV steroids |

Symptom-based first line (see flag — reads like IBS management): pain/bloating → antispasmodic (Mebeverine); constipation → laxatives, avoid lactulose (Linaclotide if others failed / 12 months); diarrhoea → Loperamide; 2nd line low-dose TCA; CBT if 12 months; prevention with Azathioprine if 2+ exacerbations/year.

Diet: regular meals; ~8 cups fluid/day; tea/coffee ≤3 cups/day; reduce alcohol & fizzy drinks; limit high-fibre foods and resistant starch; fresh fruit ≤3/day; avoid sorbitol for diarrhoea; increase oats for bloating

- Affects terminal ileum and colon (anywhere mouth to anus)
- Increased relapse risk with NSAIDs and cOCP; non-caseating granulomas

Investigations:
- Colonoscopy is the choice (deep ulcers, skip lesions)
- Biopsy: deep infiltrate mucosa to muscularis propria with granulomata/goblet cells
- **Small bowel enema: Kantor's string sign, rose thorn ulcers**
- CRP correlates with activity; bile-acid malabsorption → SeHCAT test

Inducing remission: glucocorticoids (or budesonide); enteral feeding with elemental diet (esp if young); 5-ASA (Mesalazine) 2nd line [S/E interstitial nephritis, headaches, acute pancreatitis, agranulocytosis]; Infliximab if refractory; Metronidazole if isolated peri-anal; smoking cessation

Maintaining remission: Azathioprine and Mercaptopurine first-line (assess TPMT first — genetic deficiency → severe S/E; if below normal but not deficient, lower dose; MTX 2nd line). Post-resection diarrhoea with normal CRP → Cholestyramine

Surgery: 80% eventually need it; stricturing terminal ileal → ileocaecal resection; segmental resections; stricturoplasty; perianal fistulae → MRI then draining (Flagyl first); perianal abscess → I&D + ABx

Complications: small bowel cancer 40%; colorectal cancer 2%; osteoporosis

Sulfasalazine (IBD and RA): S/E male infertility, skin rashes, diarrhoea, visual disturbances

- Characterised by diarrhoea, steatorrhoea, weight loss
- Caused by intestinal, pancreatic, or biliary problems

| Intestinal | Pancreatic | Biliary |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Coeliac | Chronic pancreatitis | Biliary obstruction |
| Crohn's | Cystic fibrosis | PBC |
| Tropical sprue | Pancreatic cancer | — |
| Whipple's, giardiasis, short bowel, lactase insufficiency, bacterial overgrowth, lymphoma | — | — |

Coeliac disease:
- Associated with HLA-DQ2, anaemia, low ferritin
- Investigation: TTG
- Complications: osteoporosis, lactose intolerance, subfertility, oesophageal cancer, hyposplenism → needs pneumococcal vaccine
- Must check **IgA (which can give a false positive TTG result)**
- Avoid beer, rye, bread, pasta

Whipple's disease:
- Bacterial infection by Tropheryma whipplei; middle-aged men; HLA-B27
- Sx: joint pain, diarrhoea → malabsorption (small intestine involvement)
- CT: mesenteric lymphadenopathy
- Jejunal biopsy: macrophages containing **PAS granules**
- Managed with oral co-trimoxazole for a year

Systemic sclerosis: autoimmune fibrosis; affects GIT in 80% → malabsorption
Cystic fibrosis: pancreatic insufficiency → poor digestion/absorption

- Villous atrophy associations: tropical sprue, coeliac, Whipple's, hypogammaglobulinaemia, lymphomas
- **Small bowel overgrowth: hydrogen breath test (first-line test)**; treat with rifaximin
- TPN: LFT derangement; complication superficial thrombophlebitis
- Refeeding syndrome: hallmark hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia; torsades des pointes secondary to hypomagnesaemia; management reduce feed and replace; prevention provide only 50% of normal energy/protein for 2 days
- MUST screening tool: malnutrition; Waterlow: pressure sores
- Bariatric surgery: laparoscopic adjustable gastric banding (BMI 30-39); biliopancreatic diversion with duodenal switch (BMI >60, most likely to cause malabsorption)
- PEG not recommended in advanced dementia (continue to encourage oral food/fluids)

| Brush border enzyme | Breakdown product |
| --- | --- |
| Maltase | glucose + glucose |
| Sucrase | glucose + fructose |
| Lactase | glucose + galactose |
| Amylase | **breaks down starch into sugars** |

Acute pancreatitis causes — GET SMASHED:
- Gallstones, Ethanol, Trauma, Steroids, Mumps, Autoimmune/Ascaris, Scorpion venom, **Hypertriglyceridaemia**/chylomicronaemia/hypercalcaemia, ERCP, Drugs (**Azathioprine**, Mesalazine, **Didanosine**, Bendroflumethiazide, Furosemide, Pentamidine, Sodium Valproate)
- **Severity assessed by hypocalcaemia or CRP**
- Pregnancy pancreatitis most likely due to gallstones
- Amylase is one useful for diagnosis

Complications:
- Peripancreatic fluid collections in 25% (can develop into pseudocyst/abscess); self-resolving; haemorrhage
- Pseudocyst: 2-4 weeks post-pancreatitis; 75% with amylase elevation; Ix CT/ERCP/MRI/EUS; observe 12 weeks (50% self-resolve); treat with cystogastrostomy/aspiration
- Pancreatic abscess (infected pseudocyst): drainage
- Pancreatic necrosis: early necrosectomy high mortality (avoided unless indicated); sterile necrosis conservative; ARDS ~20% mortality
- Purtscher retinopathy: cotton wool spots; common after acute pancreatitis

Chronic pancreatitis: 80% from alcohol excess; abdominal pain, bulky stools, weight loss; faecal elastase (exocrine function); Ix CT abdomen

- **I cells in upper small intestine → produce CCK**
- **Colicky RUQ pain after eating a fatty meal (biliary colic)**
- Palpable RUQ mass (gallbladder) → Courvoisier's sign — seen in cholangiocarcinoma if painless jaundice
- Double duct dilatation (CBD & pancreatic) → pancreatic cancer
- Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (fatal): epigastric pain, profuse vomiting, confusion, hypoglycaemia, brisk reflexes

- Gram +ve exotoxin → pseudomembranous colitis
- Highly associated with clindamycin (classic); more recently 2nd/3rd-gen cephalosporins; PPIs also a RF
- Transmission via faecal-oral route by **ingestion of spores**
- Raised WBC is characteristic
- Recurrence 20%, and 50% after 2nd episode

Management:
- First line: oral vancomycin
- Second line: oral fidaxomicin
- Life threatening: **oral vancomycin + IV metronidazole**
- Recurrence <12 weeks → oral fidaxomicin; >12 weeks → oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin
- FMT for 2+ episodes; prevention Bezlotoxumab (if history and currently on ABx)
- Isolation in side room until no diarrhoea for at least 48h

| Organism | Clue |
| --- | --- |
| Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) | Perianal itching in children (nursery) |
| Ancylostoma duodenale (hookworm) | Abdominal pain, diarrhoea, weight loss, fatigue; barefoot walking |
| Ascaris lumbricoides (roundworm) | Tropical/subtropical, poor sanitation |
| Strongyloides stercoralis (threadworm) | Faecal contamination of soil/water |
| Taenia solium (pork tapeworm) | Undercooked pork / eggs |

- Hydatid cyst (E. granulosus): **Surgical excision**; **Albendazole pre- and post-operatively to reduce recurrence**
- Hep B: safe for mothers to breastfeed (not transmitted through breastmilk)

| Cell / hormone | Action |
| --- | --- |
| **I cells (upper small intestine)** | **produce CCK** |
| **S cells (upper small intestine)** | **Secretin** — **increases bicarbonate-rich fluid** from pancreas & hepatic duct cells |
| D cells (pancreas & stomach) | **Somatostatin** — inhibits gastrin; symptoms of carcinoid (diarrhoea, flushing) |
| G cells (antrum of stomach) | Gastrin — increases HCl (parietal cells) and gastric motility; stimulated by luminal peptides |
| VIP | Most arise from pancreas, some from small intestine |

Carcinoid: flushing (first symptom), diarrhoea, bronchospasm, tricuspid stenosis (soft mid-diastolic murmur), pellagra (vit B3/niacin deficiency). Urinary 5-HIAA. Affects the right side of the heart (pulmonary stenosis, tricuspid insufficiency)

| Drug | Note |
| --- | --- |
| PPI side effects | **Hyponatraemia**, **hypomagnesaemia**; **osteoporosis** (fracture risk); **microscopic** colitis (colonoscopy); C. diff |
| Drugs causing dyspepsia | Isosorbide mononitrate; Prednisolone; Aminophylline; Amlodipine |

Miscellaneous high-yield:
- Wiskott-Aldrich: X-linked, eczema, recurrent infections, thrombocytopenia
- Friedreich's ataxia: ataxia, dysarthria, loss of reflexes, HOCM
- Acute intermittent porphyria: abdominal pain, psychiatric disturbances

cards.q: Haemochromatosis: how do you screen, and what test for family members?
cards.a: Screen with transferrin saturation and ferritin (transferrin sat higher than ferritin). For family members: **HFE** genetic testing.

cards.q: Haemochromatosis (C282Y homozygous): first- and second-line management?
cards.a: First line: venesection (weekly, then monthly once levels below 50). Second line: Desferrioxamine. Only cardiomyopathy and skin pigmentation are reversible.

cards.q: Wilson's disease: inheritance and gene?
cards.a: **AR** disorder — ATP7B gene mutation; impaired copper excretion. Young patient with hepatic + neurological features.

cards.q: Wilson's disease: characteristic copper studies?
cards.a: **Low caeruloplasmin and low total serum copper.** Also Kayser-Fleischer rings.

cards.q: PBC: classic autoimmune association (bolded)?
cards.a: **Sjogren's in 80%** (also RA, systemic sclerosis, thyroid disease). Middle-aged females (9:1 F:M).

cards.q: PBC: which ducts are damaged?
cards.a: **Interlobular bile ducts** damaged by chronic inflammation → progressive cholestasis, may progress to cirrhosis.

cards.q: PBC: classic symptom and high-yield skin finding?
cards.a: Classic feature: **pruritus**. Also **Xanthelasma** (plus xanthomata, clubbing, hepatosplenomegaly).

cards.q: PBC: characteristic raised immunoglobulin and key antibody?
cards.a: Raised serum **IgM**; anti-mitochondrial Ab M2 subtype in 98%. 1st line treatment ursodeoxycholic acid.

cards.q: PSC: which bile ducts are involved (bolded)?
cards.a: **Intra and extra hepatic bile ducts** — inflammation and fibrosis of unknown aetiology.

cards.q: PSC: key associations (bolded)?
cards.a: **UC** (80% of PSC have UC) and **also associated with HIV**. MRCP shows beaded strictures; cholangiocarcinoma in 10%.

cards.q: Cirrhotic ascites: which diuretic class is given (bolded)?
cards.a: **Aldosterone antagonist (Spironolactone)** — add a loop diuretic if no response.

cards.q: SBP prophylaxis: when and with what (bolded)?
cards.a: **Cipro if cirrhosis and ascites with protein of 15 g/L.**

cards.q: SAAG >11 g/L vs <11 g/L — what does each mean?
cards.a: >11 = portal hypertension / transudate (liver disease MC — alcohol, ALF, mets; RHF; Budd-Chiari). <11 = exudate (nephrotic, HCC, malignancy, TB peritonitis, pancreatitis).

cards.q: Hepatorenal syndrome: treatment and mechanism (bolded)?
cards.a: Terlipressin (vasopressin analogue) → **splanchnic vasoconstriction** to reduce portal pressure and improve renal blood flow.

cards.q: HCC: most common cause in the UK (bolded)?
cards.a: **MC cause of HCC in the UK is Hep C.**

cards.q: Autoimmune hepatitis: key labs (bolded)?
cards.a: **IgG elevated / smooth muscle antibodies.**

cards.q: SBP: first-line antibiotic and MC organism?
cards.a: IV Cefotaxime (or any 3rd-gen cephalosporin). MC organism: E. coli.

cards.q: Achalasia: dysphagia pattern, investigations, treatment?
cards.a: Dysphagia to BOTH solids and liquids. Manometry (excessive tone), barium swallow (bird's beak), fluid level on CXR. Treatment: balloon dilation.

cards.q: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome: diagnostic test (bolded)?
cards.a: **Fasting gastrin levels with secretin stimulation test.** MC site: first part of duodenum; 30% MEN I.

cards.q: UGIB: which blood result correlates with an upper source, and which scores are used?
cards.a: High urea correlates with UGIB. Glasgow-Blatchford (pre-endoscopy, OPAT vs inpatient); Rockall (post-endoscopy, rebleeding/mortality). Endoscopy within 24h.

cards.q: Variceal haemorrhage: the four bolded high-priority points?
cards.a: **Terlipressin**; prophylactic ABx to **reduce mortality**; endoscopic variceal **band ligation**; **TIPSS** (if all else fails).

cards.q: Oesophageal/gastric cancer: what assesses mural invasion (bolded)?
cards.a: **Endoscopic ultrasound is used to assess mural invasion.**

cards.q: Barrett's oesophagus: pathology and management?
cards.a: Metaplasia of lower oesophageal mucosa to columnar epithelium (GORD → Barrett's → adenocarcinoma). High-dose PPIs + endoscopic surveillance. Gold standard reflux test: 24h pH monitoring.

cards.q: Eosinophilic oesophagitis: key management (bolded)?
cards.a: **Fluticasone** (swallowed topical corticosteroids); also PPI and six-food elimination diet.

cards.q: Colon cancer: sporadic mutation pathway and commonest inherited type?
cards.a: Sporadic: allelic loss of APC, p53 deletion, DCC deletion, K-ras activation. MC inherited colorectal Ca: HNPCC. Associated with endometrial cancer.

cards.q: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome: inheritance and hallmark (bolded)?
cards.a: **AD disease with development of non-cancerous hamartomatous polyps in the GI tract.** Hyperpigmented lip/oral macules; 50% die from cancer by 60.

cards.q: Anal cancer: key association (bolded)?
cards.a: **Anal cancer is strongly associated with HPV16.**

cards.q: Co-Danthramer: key prescribing trap (bolded)?
cards.a: **Only prescribed to palliative patients** due to its **carcinogenic potential** (severe opioid-induced constipation not responding).

cards.q: UC: Truelove and Witts severity by stools/day?
cards.a: <4 = mild: oral aminosalicylate. 4-6 = moderate: topical rectal aminosalicylates. >6 (+pyrexia, tachycardia, anaemia, raised markers) = severe: IV steroids. Flare triggered by smoking cessation.

cards.q: Crohn's disease: small bowel enema findings (bolded)?
cards.a: **Small bowel enema: Kantor's string sign, rose thorn ulcers.** Non-caseating granulomas; colonoscopy is Ix of choice.

cards.q: Crohn's: maintaining remission and the test before starting?
cards.a: Azathioprine/Mercaptopurine first-line; assess TPMT first (genetic deficiency → severe S/E). MTX 2nd line.

cards.q: Coeliac disease: why must you also check IgA (bolded)?
cards.a: Association with **IgA (which can give a false positive TTG result).** Coeliac: HLA-DQ2, TTG; hyposplenism → pneumococcal vaccine.

cards.q: Whipple's disease: hallmark biopsy finding (bolded)?
cards.a: Jejunal biopsy — macrophages containing **PAS granules.** Tropheryma whipplei; treat with oral co-trimoxazole for a year.

cards.q: SIBO: first-line test and treatment (bolded)?
cards.a: **Hydrogen breath test (first-line test).** Treatment: rifaximin.

cards.q: Amylase: what does it break down (bolded)?
cards.a: **Amylase breaks down starch into sugars.**

cards.q: Acute pancreatitis: bolded high-yield causes and severity assessment?
cards.a: **Hypertriglyceridaemia**; drugs **Azathioprine**, **Didanosine**. **Severity assessed by hypocalcaemia or CRP.** Causes: GET SMASHED.

cards.q: Biliary colic: which cell produces CCK and the classic pain (bolded)?
cards.a: **I cells in upper small intestine produce CCK.** **Colicky RUQ pain after eating a fatty meal (biliary colic).**

cards.q: Courvoisier's sign and double duct dilatation — significance?
cards.a: Palpable RUQ mass (gallbladder) = Courvoisier's, seen in cholangiocarcinoma if painless jaundice. Double duct (CBD & pancreatic) = pancreatic cancer.

cards.q: C. difficile: transmission and life-threatening management (bolded)?
cards.a: Transmission by **ingestion of spores.** Life-threatening: **oral vancomycin + IV metronidazole.** First line oral vancomycin, 2nd line fidaxomicin.

cards.q: Hydatid cyst (E. granulosus): management (bolded)?
cards.a: **Surgical excision** with **Albendazole pre- and post-operatively to reduce recurrence.**

cards.q: GI hormone cells: I, S, D — hormones and actions (bolded)?
cards.a: **I → CCK**; **S → Secretin** (**increases bicarbonate-rich fluid**); **D → Somatostatin** (inhibits gastrin). G cells (antrum) → gastrin.

cards.q: Carcinoid syndrome: features and investigation?
cards.a: Flushing (first symptom), diarrhoea, bronchospasm, tricuspid stenosis, pellagra (B3/niacin). Urinary 5-HIAA. Affects right side of the heart.

cards.q: PPI side effects (bolded high-yield)?
cards.a: **Hyponatraemia** and **hypomagnesaemia**; **osteoporosis** (raised fracture risk); **microscopic** colitis; C. diff.

cards.q: GI parasites — organism to clinical clue?
cards.a: Enterobius (pinworm): perianal itching in children. Ancylostoma (hookworm): barefoot walking. Ascaris (roundworm): poor sanitation. Strongyloides: faecal soil/water. Taenia solium: undercooked pork.
